common_name: Dunstan Hybrid Chestnut (American-Chinese Hybrid Chestnut, Blight-Resistant Eastern Chestnut)

planting.note: Plant bare-root or container trees in early spring in well-drained, slightly acidic (pH 5.0–6.5), deep, sandy loam or loamy soil in full sun; Dunstan Hybrid Chestnut is self-fertile and can produce a crop as a single tree, though yields improve with cross-pollination from another chestnut variety; avoid heavy clay or waterlogged sites — chestnut does not tolerate wet feet and is susceptible to Phytophthora root rot in poorly drained conditions; does NOT require the blight fungus management needed for American chestnut.

directions: Plant in well-drained, acidic, sandy loam or loamy soil in full sun; Dunstan Hybrid Chestnut is self-fertile; mulch heavily to maintain soil moisture and acidity; harvest nuts in September–October when burrs split open and chestnuts fall; gather from ground promptly as ground moisture causes rapid spoilage; refrigerate fresh nuts within days of harvest since they are perishable. Dunstan Hybrid Chestnut represents one of the most important advances in American chestnut restoration, developed by Dr. Robert Dunstan of Greensboro, North Carolina in the 1950s and 1960s after discovering a surviving American chestnut tree in Ohio (the Graves tree) that appeared to have some natural resistance to the chestnut blight (Cryphonectria parasitica) that wiped out approximately 4 billion American chestnut trees from the eastern forest between 1904 and 1950. Dunstan crossed this apparently blight-resistant American chestnut with Chinese chestnut (Castanea mollissima), which has co-evolved with the blight fungus in Asia and carries true genetic resistance, to produce a hybrid combining the large size and straight timber form approaching American chestnut with the blight resistance of the Chinese parent. The Dunstan Hybrid produced nuts with excellent size and sweet flavor — closer to American chestnut sweetness than to the less-flavorful Chinese chestnut — while demonstrating substantially better blight resistance than standard American chestnut. Dunstan Hybrids have been widely planted in the southeastern and Mid-Atlantic states for orchard nut production, wildlife habitat (deer, turkey, and squirrels relish chestnuts), and as a productive native-type chestnut for home orchards where the formerly dominant American chestnut once grew.
sources: Chestnut Hill Outdoors — Dunstan Hybrid Chestnut documentation
USDA National Agroforestry Center — Chestnut Production

common_name: Colossal Chestnut (European-Japanese Hybrid Chestnut, California Large Chestnut, Giant Chestnut)

planting.note: Plant bare-root or container trees in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic (pH 5.5–6.5), deep, fertile loam in full sun; Colossal requires cross-pollination from another chestnut variety for good nut set — plant with another Colossal or with a compatible European-Japanese hybrid; the variety is adapted to the mild-winter climates of California, Oregon, and Washington (zones 6–9) but is less cold-hardy than Dunstan or Chinese chestnut, performing best where winter temperatures rarely drop below 0°F.

directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic, fertile loam in full sun; Colossal requires cross-pollination; harvest nuts in October when burrs open and nuts fall naturally; collect promptly from ground; Colossal chestnuts are among the largest available — single nuts can reach 30+ grams, comparable to the best Italian and French chestnuts — with sweet, creamy flesh excellent for roasting, marrons glacés, and fresh cooking. Colossal chestnut is a hybrid of European sweet chestnut (Castanea sativa) and Japanese chestnut (Castanea crenata) developed in California in the late 1800s by Felix Gillet, a French-born nurseryman who operated the Nevada City Nursery in northern California and imported and trialed numerous European and Asian chestnut varieties and crosses. The variety became the standard commercial chestnut in California and the Pacific Northwest, where it grows vigorously in the mild, well-drained soils of the region and produces exceptionally large individual nuts — among the largest of any named chestnut variety, comparable to the finest Italian and French chestnuts in size and flavor. The large nut size (a single nut can weigh an ounce or more) and good flavor — sweet, starchy, and rich — make Colossal the variety of choice for specialty fresh-market chestnut sales, marron glacé (candied whole chestnuts), and roasting. It has been widely planted in California's chestnut-growing regions (Nevada County, the Sierra Nevada foothills) and in Oregon and Washington for commercial and home production.
sources: University of California Cooperative Extension — Chestnut Production
Oregon State University Extension — Nut Tree Production
California Rare Fruit Growers — Chestnut Varieties

common_name: Chojuro Asian Pear (Brown Russet Japanese Pear, Butterscotch Asian Pear)

planting.note: Plant bare-root or container trees in early spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile, deep soil in full sun; Chojuro requires cross-pollination from another Asian pear variety — Hosui, Shinseiki, or Nijisseiki are compatible; Chojuro is one of the oldest named Japanese pear varieties still in commercial production, noted for distinctive butterscotch and caramel notes in the flavor that no other variety replicates.

directions: Plant in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Chojuro requires a cross-pollinator; harvest in late August to mid-September when the skin color transitions from greenish-brown to warm brown-russet; Chojuro stores well under refrigeration (2–3 months) longer than many other Asian pears; flavor improves slightly with 2–4 weeks of cold storage after harvest. Chojuro is one of the oldest named Asian pear varieties in continuous cultivation, developed in Japan from a cross of two traditional Japanese pear varieties in the late 1800s and widely grown there for well over a century before introduction to North American home orchards. The variety's distinguishing characteristic is a unique flavor profile — described as butterscotch, caramel, or anise-like by different tasters — that is completely unlike any other Asian pear variety and arguably more complex than the straightforward crisp sweetness of varieties like Shinseiki and Hosui. The nuts are medium to large, round to oblate, with a rich brown-russet skin that is slightly rough in texture, and crisp, dense, white flesh with the characteristic refreshing juiciness of Asian pears combined with the distinctive butterscotch flavor undertone. Chojuro has become popular among specialty Asian pear growers and farmers' market vendors precisely because its unusual flavor creates a point of differentiation from the more neutral-flavored standard varieties — customers who taste it for the first time often find the flavor complex and memorable. Good fire blight resistance compared to some other varieties.
sources: Oregon State University Extension — Asian Pear Varieties
Washington State University Extension — Tree Fruit Production

common_name: Korean Giant Asian Pear (Olympic Pear, Jumbo Asian Pear, Large Korean Pear)

planting.note: Plant bare-root or container trees in early spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile, deep soil in full sun; Korean Giant requires cross-pollination from another Asian pear variety — Hosui, Chojuro, or Shinseiki; Korean Giant ripens very late (September–October in most locations), later than most other Asian pears, requiring a long growing season; it is one of the largest Asian pears available — individual fruits commonly weigh 1–2 lbs.

directions: Plant in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Korean Giant requires a cross-pollinator; thin fruit clusters in May to 1 fruit per spur for maximum fruit development — this variety has the capacity to produce extremely large fruit when properly thinned; harvest in late September to October when skin color shifts from green to yellow-green and fruit gives very slightly to pressure. Korean Giant (also marketed as 'Olympic Pear' in American nursery trade, named for the 1988 Seoul Olympics) is a Korean variety that produces some of the largest Asian pear fruits available to home growers and specialty markets — single fruits commonly weigh 1–1.5 lbs and exceptional fruits can reach 2 lbs, making them dramatically larger than the typical 6–10 oz fruits of Hosui, Shinseiki, and Nijisseiki. The size is the variety's primary market distinction: Korean Giant pears make a striking visual impression at farmers' markets and gift fruit presentations, where the giant, pale gold-green to golden-brown skin and exceptional juicy-sweet flavor create a memorable product. The flavor at peak ripeness is excellent — sweet, refreshingly crisp, and juicy with a clean, mild pear flavor — and the very late ripening season (September–October) extends the fresh Asian pear harvest window well after early and mid-season varieties have finished. Korean Giant requires careful fruit thinning to develop full-sized individual fruits; without thinning, trees set too many fruits and individual nuts remain smaller.
sources: Oregon State University Extension — Asian Pear Varieties
Washington State University Tree Fruit Research and Extension Center

common_name: Kosui Asian Pear (Early Sweet Japanese Pear, Small Golden Russet Asian Pear)

planting.note: Plant bare-root or container trees in early spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile soil in full sun; Kosui requires cross-pollination from a compatible variety — Hosui or Chojuro are recommended companions with overlapping bloom times; Kosui is an early-season Asian pear (ripening in late July to August), providing ripe Asian pears several weeks before mid- and late-season varieties, giving it value as a season-extender from the beginning of the harvest window.

directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic, fertile soil in full sun; Kosui requires a cross-pollinator; harvest in late July to August when the golden-brown russet skin reaches full color and fruit gives very slightly under thumb pressure; Kosui has somewhat shorter shelf life than later-season varieties — best consumed within 2–4 weeks of harvest; the small to medium size makes it ideal for single-serving eating straight off the tree. Kosui is a widely grown Japanese pear variety developed through deliberate cross-breeding at the National Institute of Fruit Tree Science in Japan and released in 1959, becoming one of the most popular fresh-eating Asian pear varieties in Japan and increasingly common in American specialty orchards and Asian-American community gardens. The variety's most important attribute for home orchardists is its early ripening season — Kosui typically ripens in late July to August in the Pacific Northwest and mid-Atlantic, 3–6 weeks before mid-season varieties like Hosui and 6–8 weeks before Korean Giant, allowing fresh Asian pear harvest to begin at the height of summer rather than early fall. The fruits are small to medium-sized with golden-brown russet skin and crisp, juicy, very sweet white flesh with a clean, refreshing flavor and aromatic quality. The sweetness and early ripening of Kosui — combined with its thin, tender skin that doesn't need peeling — make it an appealing variety for fresh eating directly from the tree. Productive and reliable in zones 5–9.
sources: Oregon State University Extension — Asian Pear Varieties
National Institute of Fruit Tree Science Japan — Kosui Variety Notes
Washington State University Tree Fruit Research and Extension Center
